name: Pixie
description: Pixies are mythical creatures of British folklore, particularly prominent in Devon and Cornwall. They are generally depicted as small, human-like beings with pointed ears and a mischievous nature. Often associated with wild places such as moors, forests, and stone circles, pixies are said to be fond of playing pranks on humans, leading travelers astray, tangling the manes of horses, and stealing small items. While not inherently malevolent, they can be dangerous if angered or disrespected. Their origins are debated, with some scholars suggesting connections to pre-Christian beliefs and spirits of the land. They are often conflated with fairies, though some distinctions exist, particularly in regional folklore. Pixies are frequently described as having a love for music, dancing, and revelry, and are believed to possess magical abilities related to illusion and enchantment. They remain a popular figure in modern fantasy literature and art, often representing the untamed and whimsical aspects of the natural world. Some accounts ascribe to them a guarding role of ancient sites and natural treasures.
subtitle: Mischievous spirits of the British countryside
appearance: Small, human-like beings with pointed ears, often dressed in green or other earth-toned clothing. May have wings resembling those of butterflies or dragonflies. Their size varies depending on the region and the story, but they are generally depicted as being no more than a few inches to a foot tall.
habitat: Forests, moors, stone circles, and other wild or secluded places, particularly in Devon and Cornwall.
behavior: Mischievous and playful, often playing pranks on humans. Can be helpful or harmful depending on their mood and how they are treated. Fond of music, dancing, and revelry. Sometimes lead travelers astray or steal small items.

keyMyths.title: Pixie-Led
keyMyths.description: One of the most common tales involving pixies is that of being 'pixie-led,' where a traveler becomes lost or disoriented due to the pixies' magic. The pixies create illusions or subtly alter the landscape, leading the person in circles or into dangerous areas. The only way to escape this enchantment is to turn your coat inside out or carry iron.

keyMyths.title: The Pixies' Revenge
keyMyths.description: Stories abound of pixies taking revenge on those who offend them. This might involve causing misfortune to a farmer who disrespects their land, or playing tricks on someone who mocks their existence. The revenge is often subtle and persistent, creating a sense of unease and bad luck for the offender until amends are made.

keyMyths.title: Pixies and the Church
keyMyths.description: A legend tells of pixies helping to build a church. One version suggests they provided the stones, while another says they helped guide the workers. However, after the church was completed, the pixies vanished, feeling their wild nature was incompatible with the structured order of the church. This highlights the perceived tension between the natural and the religious world.
keyMyths.source: Various local church histories in Devon

sources.author: Katherine Briggs
sources.description: A comprehensive resource on British folklore, including detailed entries on pixies and their associated beliefs and traditions.
sources.text: A comprehensive resource on British folklore, including detailed entries on pixies and their associated beliefs and traditions.

sources.author: Theo Brown
sources.description: A collection of folklore and local stories from Dartmoor, Devon, featuring numerous accounts of pixies and their interactions with the local community.
sources.text: A collection of folklore and local stories from Dartmoor, Devon, featuring numerous accounts of pixies and their interactions with the local community.

sources.author: Jennifer Westwood
sources.work: The Lore of Scotland: A Guide to Scottish Legends
sources.description: While focused on Scotland, this work provides valuable context for understanding the broader Celtic and British folklore traditions that inform pixie lore.
